items.q: What drives water up the xylem in a tall tree?
items.correct: Transpiration pull — evaporation from leaves creates tension that pulls water up the continuous water column.
items.misconception: Roots actively pump water up to the leaves like a heart.
items.random_wrong: Capillary action alone is enough to lift water to the top of any tree.
items.why: Students assume there must be an active 'pump' system like circulation in animals; they miss that the evaporation at the top does the work.

items.q: Where do plants get most of their biomass from?
items.correct: From carbon dioxide in the air — the carbon in plant tissue comes mostly from CO2 fixed during photosynthesis.
items.misconception: Plants get most of their mass from the soil through their roots.
items.random_wrong: Plants gain most of their mass directly from sunlight, which has weight.
items.why: Roots in soil look like the source of food/mass, but actually most plant dry mass is carbon from atmospheric CO2.

items.q: In osmosis, which direction does water move?
items.correct: From a region of higher water potential (less concentrated solution) to lower water potential.
items.misconception: Water moves from high solute concentration to low solute concentration.
items.random_wrong: Water moves towards the sugar because sugar attracts water molecules chemically.
items.why: Students get confused between solute concentration and water potential; they flip the direction because 'high concentration' feels like it should attract.

items.q: Does the xylem carry sugars made in the leaves?
items.correct: No — the xylem carries water and minerals up from the roots; the phloem transports sugars made in the leaves.
items.misconception: Xylem transports the sugars produced by photosynthesis.
items.random_wrong: Xylem and phloem both carry only water upwards.
items.why: Students swap the two tissues; remember xylem = water up, phloem = food (sugars) around the plant by translocation.

items.q: Do plants take in carbon dioxide only and never give it out?
items.correct: No — plants respire all the time, releasing CO₂; in the light they also photosynthesise, taking in CO₂.
items.misconception: Plants only take in carbon dioxide and never release it.
items.random_wrong: Plants do not respire because they make their own food.
items.why: Students see only photosynthesis, but plants respire continuously like all organisms, so the net gas exchange depends on light intensity.

items.q: Which structure absorbs light energy in plants?
items.why: Correct answer: Chlorophyll. “Cellulose” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which tissue carries sugars around a plant?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Phloem. “Xylem” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many CO₂ molecules make one glucose in photosynthesis?
items.why: The real figure is 6. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
